skill.name: dittobot
skill.description: Edit, rewrite, tighten, diagnose, and adapt user-provided prose while preserving voice, facts, intent, stance, rhythm, humor, and formality.
skill.summary: Voice-faithful editing for drafts, notes, emails, posts, and other user-provided prose.
skill.author: RegionallyFamous

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many issues, but most markdown, license, YAML, and hash-related hits are false positives. Confirmed risks come from optional installer and evaluation scripts that download archives, modify the local skills directory, create symlinks, run subprocesses, and call the OpenAI API when a key is present. No evidence found of malicious intent or prompt injection, but publication should include a warning about running optional scripts and live evaluations.

medium_findings.title: Installer Downloads Code And Modifies Local Skill Files
medium_findings.description: The README recommends a curl-to-bash terminal fallback, and install.sh downloads a repository archive before running scripts/install.py. The Python installer copies files, moves existing installs to backups, and can create a symlink in the user skills directory. This is legitimate installer behavior, but it is a supply-chain and filesystem risk if users run it without reviewing the source.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence shows download, extraction, file copy, move, and symlink operations. The behavior appears purposeful for installation, not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Optional Live Evaluation Sends Prompts And Bearer Token To Network Endpoint
medium_findings.description: scripts/live_eval.py reads OPENAI_API_KEY, builds an Authorization header, and posts selected prompts plus the skill text to the Responses API. It refuses custom API URLs unless an explicit allow flag is passed, which reduces exfiltration risk, but private draft text can still leave the local machine during live evaluation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network request and bearer token header are explicit. Safeguards are present, so this is a legitimate opt-in risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Private Draft And Transcript Handling Requires User Care
medium_findings.description: The security guide warns that live-eval transcripts, local profile cards, ledgers, and writing samples can contain private text. live_eval.py provides raw-source and raw-output save options, and redact_case.py processes secrets and private content for public-safe reports. These tools are useful but can expose sensitive writing if misused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The repository explicitly documents private-text risks and ships redaction/transcript tooling. The risk depends on user choices, so confidence is high for sensitivity but not for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives For Hashing And Prose
low_findings.description: Several weak-cryptography alerts point to natural language, license text, or SHA-256 hashing used for integrity and reporting. No evidence found that weak algorithms are used for password storage, encryption, signing, or security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations show metadata, GPL prose, or SHA-256 digest helpers. These do not match unsafe cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Documentation Backticks And Printed Commands Are Not Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command alerts are markdown examples or command strings printed for users to review. These locations do not execute user-controlled shell input by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed examples are documentation or printed shell snippets. They are not invoked through shell=True or similar execution paths.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found In Reviewed Skill Text
low_findings.description: Reviewed skill and reference files did not contain instructions to override the evaluator, skip security review, claim pre-approval, or impersonate system messages. The skill includes user-facing fence syntax, but it is framed as editing constraints for user drafts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found constraint-handling instructions, not evaluator override language. Confidence is limited to reviewed files and static-analysis paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Curl Pipe To Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The README includes a curl-to-bash terminal fallback. This pattern executes remote content and should be used only after source review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the README. It is a known supply-chain risk even when the repository intent is benign.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Network Request
dangerous_patterns.description: The live evaluation script sends an Authorization bearer token to the configured API endpoint when OPENAI_API_KEY is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Authorization header and urllib request are explicit. URL validation reduces but does not remove operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Install Move And Symlink Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer can move an existing skill directory, install a copied package, or create a symlink to the repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is directly visible. It is expected installer functionality but can affect user files.

content.user_title: Rewrite Prose in the User Voice
content.value_statement: Generic AI edits often flatten voice, add unsupported facts, or make drafts too polished. Dittobot helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code revise user-provided prose while preserving meaning, stance, and style.
actual_capabilities: Rewrites messy notes, emails, posts, captions, bios, essays, and internal documents.
Preserves facts, claims, uncertainty, names, dates, quotes, and explicit constraints.
Offers proofread, light edit, tighten, rewrite, punch up, diagnosis, options, and voice profile modes.
Uses optional fact fences for protected claims, voice markers, avoided terms, and boundaries.
Includes local regression, audit, scorecard, redaction, and live-evaluation scripts for maintainers.
limitations: Works best when the user provides source text or a clear writing sample.
Does not verify facts, legal claims, medical claims, citations, or external evidence.
Optional scripts can access files, run subprocesses, and call external APIs when invoked.
Voice profiles transfer editing taste only; they should not store private facts.

use_cases.title: Polish Work Messages Without Losing Tone
use_cases.description: Turn rough emails, Slack updates, and announcements into clear prose that still sounds like the sender.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Public Writing Drafts
use_cases.description: Tighten posts, essays, bios, captions, and website copy while keeping the original stance and rhythm.

use_cases.title: Maintain A Reusable Voice Profile
use_cases.description: Infer compact editing rules from samples so future rewrites preserve recurring voice choices without importing old facts.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Rewrite
prompt_templates.prompt: Use $dittobot on this. Keep my meaning and voice, but make it clearer and shorter: [paste draft]

prompt_templates.title: Tone Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare several versions for a sensitive message
prompt_templates.prompt: Use $dittobot on this. Give me three versions: cleaner, warmer, and sharper. Preserve all facts: [paste draft]

prompt_templates.title: Fact-Fenced Edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use $dittobot on this with these fences: [[keep: 10 business days, not counsel]] [[avoid: guaranteed]] [paste draft]

prompt_templates.title: Voice Profile Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Use $dittobot to infer a compact voice profile from these samples, then explain when not to apply it: [paste samples]

output_examples.input: A launch note that says the importer bug is fixed, but feels too long and unclear.
output_examples.output: A shorter launch note that states the bug is fixed, explains retrying failed rows, and keeps the writer calm but specific.

output_examples.input: A generic platform sentence full of broad claims and no concrete details.
output_examples.output: A modest rewrite that removes inflated language and adds a brief note that the draft needs real specifics.

output_examples.input: A legally sensitive draft with uncertainty about a notice deadline.
output_examples.output: A careful version that preserves uncertainty, keeps the deadline language, and avoids turning the claim into legal advice.

best_practices: Provide the source text, audience, purpose, and any hard constraints before asking for a rewrite.
Use fact fences for names, numbers, legal uncertainty, protected claims, and phrases that must survive.
Review outputs for factual accuracy before sending legal, medical, financial, academic, or public-facing text.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to invent missing facts, customers, metrics, citations, or legal certainty.
Do not use old voice profiles as a source of facts for a new draft.
Do not run optional install or live-evaluation scripts without reviewing their filesystem and network behavior.

faq.question: Does Dittobot write from scratch?
faq.answer: It is mainly an editor. It works best from a draft, notes, or samples that establish the user voice.

faq.question: Can it preserve exact facts and claims?
faq.answer: Yes, it is designed to protect source facts and constraints, but users should still verify sensitive claims.

faq.question: What are fact fences?
faq.answer: Fact fences are optional markers for protected facts, required claims, voice cues, avoided terms, and boundaries.

faq.question: Does it call external services during normal use?
faq.answer: The skill instructions do not require network access. Optional live-evaluation scripts call the OpenAI API when configured.

faq.question: Can it make text less AI-sounding?
faq.answer: Yes. It removes generic filler and restores source-supported voice without adding fake errors or unsupported details.

faq.question: Is it safe for private drafts?
faq.answer: Treat private drafts as sensitive. Avoid optional raw transcript saves, and review scripts before running local tools.
